critic_raw:
Patient-level splitting is not described. The design stratifies by institution and cancer type/stage but the unit of stratification is the image, not the patient. In histopathology studies, patients routinely contribute multiple images (tissue sections, staining variants, follow-up biopsies). With 10,000 images from multiple institutions over 5 years, patient-level leakage is near-certain — the model may encounter test-set images from patients it has seen during training, where it can exploit patient-specific tissue morphology rather than generalizing across patients.

The primary metric is accuracy in a multi-class imbalanced setting. The design itself acknowledges this in the Controls section ('class imbalance is addressed via per-class metrics rather than accuracy') but uses accuracy as the primary metric anyway. In a dataset where cancer type frequency varies substantially by institution (as is realistic), a model predicting the most common cancer type achieves high accuracy without medical utility. This is not a minor metric choice concern — it actively incentivizes the model to ignore minority cancer types during optimization.

The logistic regression baseline uses default hyperparameters (L2=1.0, no tuning) while the CNN undergoes full grid search with early stopping. The design justifies this as 'avoiding the confound of manual hyperparameter tuning.' But this creates an asymmetric optimization that is not a methodological advantage — it is an undertunded baseline. A regularization strength of 1.0 may be far from optimal for high-dimensional texture features, and the baseline's performance is therefore a lower bound on what LR can achieve, not the LR ceiling.

Stain normalization via the Macenko method is applied 'consistently pre-model' but Section 3 states CNN preprocessing (per-channel intensity normalization) is 'fit exclusively on the training set.' If the Macenko normalization parameters (which require fitting a stain matrix to a reference image or training distribution) are fit on the full dataset, there is a normalization leakage that differently affects the CNN's input statistics. The design is ambiguous about whether Macenko is fit on the full dataset or training only.

McNemar's test is proposed for comparing CNN vs. baseline accuracy. McNemar's test is valid for paired proportion comparisons from the same samples — it tests whether the disagreements between two classifiers are symmetric. However, if the positive class is rare and most predictions agree (both predict the majority class), McNemar's test has very low power and may declare no significant difference even when one model is clinically meaningfully better on minority classes.
all_issues_raised: The split stratifies by image, not patient — patients contributing multiple tissue images will have images in both training and test sets, allowing the model to exploit patient-specific morphological features rather than generalizing across patients.

Accuracy is used as the primary metric in a multi-class imbalanced histopathology setting despite the design's own acknowledgment that accuracy is dominated by majority-class performance — this incentivizes ignoring rare cancer types during optimization.

The logistic regression baseline uses fixed default hyperparameters (C=1.0, no tuning) while the CNN undergoes full grid search — this deliberate asymmetry produces a lower bound on LR performance, not a fair comparison.

The Macenko stain normalization fitting procedure is ambiguous — if fit on the full dataset (including test images), normalization parameters leak test-set staining statistics into the CNN's training distribution.

McNemar's test has low power in imbalanced settings where both models predominantly agree on majority-class predictions — the test will typically fail to detect meaningful per-class differences in minority cancer type performance.
all_issues_adjudicated: The split stratifies by image, not patient — patients contributing multiple tissue images will have images in both training and test sets, allowing the model to exploit patient-specific morphological features rather than generalizing across patients.

Accuracy is used as the primary metric in a multi-class imbalanced histopathology setting despite the design's own acknowledgment that accuracy is dominated by majority-class performance — this incentivizes ignoring rare cancer types during optimization.

The logistic regression baseline uses fixed default hyperparameters (C=1.0, no tuning) while the CNN undergoes full grid search — this deliberate asymmetry produces a lower bound on LR performance, not a fair comparison.

The Macenko stain normalization fitting procedure is ambiguous — if fit on the full dataset (including test images), normalization parameters leak test-set staining statistics into the CNN's training distribution.

McNemar's test has low power in imbalanced settings where both models predominantly agree on majority-class predictions — the test will typically fail to detect meaningful per-class differences in minority cancer type performance.
